must_not_assert: a confirmed LSASS dump or credential theft (createdump was invoked with PID 632 and an LSASS-suggesting filename, but the package shows only EID1 command lines — no EID10 LSASS access and no EID11 dump-file creation)
that PID 632 is confirmed to be lsass.exe (its identity is not evidenced in the package)

the_trap: createdump.exe (a legitimate Microsoft/.NET tool) is invoked against a PID with an LSASS-suggesting output filename — a real credential-access risk — but the target PID is not identified as lsass.exe, no LSASS process-access or dump-file creation is evidenced, and the legitimate tool makes intent ambiguous; do not assert a confirmed LSASS dump or credential theft.
rationale: createdump.exe (C:\Program Files\PowerShell\7) is invoked (A0 + EV-001..004) with '-f ...dotnet-lsass.dmp 632' — an LSASS-suggesting output filename targeting PID 632. The package carries only Sysmon EID1 command lines: it does NOT identify PID 632 as lsass.exe, contains no EID10 process-access to LSASS, and does not evidence successful EID11 dump-file creation. createdump is also a legitimate crash-dump tool, so both the LSASS target and malicious intent are unconfirmed: suspicious, investigate.
